headline: Tasik Chini UNESCO Biosphere Reserve Faces Delisting Threat
context: UNESCO warned in January 2026 that Tasik Chini may lose its Biosphere Reserve status following persistent mining encroachment and water quality deterioration.

cards.big: Tasik Chini has been a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve since 2009.
cards.sub: 16 years later, UNESCO has warned of delisting due to iron ore mining within the buffer zone.

cards.big: Water quality at Tasik Chini declined from Class II to Class IV since 2012.
cards.sub: Sedimentation from mining has filled 18% of the lake's basin — reducing depth and killing lotus beds.

cards.big: 7 mining permits were issued in the buffer zone between 2014 and 2022.
cards.sub: Each permit was issued by the state without notifying the UNESCO committee.

cards.big: Jakun Orang Asli communities depend on the lake for fish and water.
cards.sub: Catch per unit effort dropped 62% since mining began. No alternative livelihood programme exists.

cards.big: A biosphere reserve with active mining permits inside it is a contradiction with a UNESCO logo.
Malaysia cannot collect UNESCO designations while undermining them with extractive permits. Each designation carries obligations. Meeting them is not optional.
